It's only in Norway that the folklore says that trolls get that big. In the rest of Scandinavia they're between 4 and 8 feet tall. And not naked with grey skin.
They wear clothes made from moss, bark and sewn together dead leaves and their skin is greenish with thick black hair. Their noses are huge, their eyes are yellow that glow in the dark and they wear jewellery; bangles, bracelets, rings and piercings in their noses and elven-like ears.
The females can use magic and the males are very broadshouldered. They hoard gold, gems and rare metals and are almost as smart as humans and they speak an old form of our language, often in poetic verse form.
They have tails that look like lions tails with a tuft of hair at its end and is long enough to wrap around its waist.
I always found this type of troll more interesting than the norweigian behemoths.

Trolls come in all sizes in Norway. It's not our fault that the largest ones have become Hollywood-ized. Nøkken, kraken, draugen, vetter, nisser, huldra, skrømt and the underground people are trolls inhabiting Midgard too, so the ones you normally see in souvenir shops are jotnar/jotun or large troll specimens.

Then you have the creatures inhabiting other realms like Åsgard (fenrir etc), but that's for another time ...

reply_content:
​@Elyrion010 I think that most of them are rooted in some paranormal experiences that, over time, grew into myths and superstitions as a respons for people to try to explain things that they found no natural explanation for.
Like how people used to explain mental illnesses as demonic possessions.
But one phenomenon in nordic folklore that shares many similarities with one phenomenon that people still report today is called Berga-tagen (translates into 'taken into the mountain').
It describes how a person would just disappear for up to a week and when they came back they were traumatised. They said that they had been kidnapped by either fairies (which also was reported in England, Ireland and Scotland well into the 1700s) or trolls.
They had been taken by force into a place that was, at first, completely dark (which made people think of the inside of mountain caverns) by beings that had put them under a spell that paralyzed their body. Then there had been all sorts of lights circling above them as the beings (described as small trolls or large fairies with large black eyes) were doing things to them.
Then they would suddenly wake up in the forrest, not knowing how they got there.
This sounds very similar to modern day reports of alien abductions. If aliens actually are visiting us and doing shit like this to people, then it's fairly safe to assume that being berga-tagen was exactly that. It was just reported in the vernacular that made sense at the time.
And it means that aliens have been here much longer than we think.

And people explaining it in a way that fits society context at that place and time. 

Most abductions are far more common and less intense ( albeit) disorienting at times, but not for pure malicious intent but rather lack of awareness of a deeper spiritual level one has yet to experience or know of. 


It’s this deeper level where I share from and clarity to be found in moments like this. 

Not that there isn’t malicious beings out there, but that’s one part of a much larger whole

reply_content:
In the summer of 1989, Italian director Claudio Fragasso brought his crew to the Beehive State, distributed an awkwardly translated script to fledgling Utah actors and within a month filmed what has come to be known as the “best worst movie.”

Called “Troll 2,” the film had no affiliation with the 1986 dark comedy “Troll” and actually had nothing to do with trolls.

Instead, “Troll 2” focuses on a suburban American family that goes on vacation to the town of Nilbog — spoiler alert, it’s goblin spelled backward — and then gets threatened by vegetarian goblins that attempt to transform them into edible plants.

comment_content: Nordic folklore is magical, we need more movies about it!
reply_content: In Troll Hunter (2010), several troll species appear. The Tusseladd is a tall, forest troll with multiple heads, used mainly for display. The Ringlefant is similar in size but less detailed. Dovregubben are medium-sized, cave-dwelling trolls with large noses and brown fur. The Jötunn (or Jotne in Norwegian) is the largest—a towering, Godzilla-like giant from Norse mythology. Each troll reflects Norwegian folklore, adapted for a modern, documentary-style story.
